Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients

**Marshmallow** is a type of confection made of stabilized aerated sugar.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

At its most basic, all marshmallow is a confection of cooked sugar that is stabilized and made fluffy by the incorporation of air. Historically, marshmallow was made from the sap of the marshmallow plant, sugar, and egg whites.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) The marshmallow sap has since been primarily replaced with gelatin in modern marshmallow,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) although other stabilizers like agar and gum arabic may also be used to stabilize the sugar foam and achieve the requisite elastic consistency.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:6-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:5-7) Depending on the formula, egg whites and albumen may or may not be included,[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) with egg white contributing a fluffier texture.[[9]](#cite_note-:9-9) A variety of sugars can be used, including table sugar (sucrose), glucose syrup, corn syrup, etc.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6)[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) Food coloring and/or additional flavoring can be added to achieve the desired final product.[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6)[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8)[[10]](#cite_note-:10-10)

![](/wiki/File:Marshmallow_fluff2.jpg)

Marshmallow creme

The exact physical characteristics depend on the ingredients and their proportions,[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) but most marshmallow tends to be light, chewy, "pillowy", and very sweet.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:5-7) Typical "firm" marshmallow has a high stabilizer (e.g. gelatin) content that gives it a soft but bouncy texture that will hold its shape. When the amount of stabilizer is reduced, the texture becomes much softer and more spreadable, making **marshmallow creme**. Increasing the sugar content and dehydrating the marshmallow produces **cereal marshmallows**, which have a very sweet flavor and dense, crunchy texture.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Marshmallow may be molded or piped into a variety of attractive shapes,[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) and the size can range from a few millimeters to a few centimeters long. It may also come with a coating for ease of handling, ranging from starch at the simplest to more flavorful ingredients like chocolate.[[7]](#cite_note-:5-7)

When exposed to direct heat, marshmallow will begin to soften and eventually caramelize, producing a sweet with additional depth of flavor.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

## Production

[edit | edit source]

### Small-scale

[edit | edit source]

![](/wiki/File:Marshmallow-Creme.jpg)

Marshmallow whipped using a stand mixer

At small scale, marshmallow is made by cooking a hot sugar syrup, mixing in gelatin and/or egg whites, and whipping in air by hand or electric mixer.[[12]](#cite_note-:4-12) This whipping incorporates into the mixture many tiny air bubbles, which are stabilized by the sugar and gelatin (or other stabilizer) and trapped after the mixture sets.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[9]](#cite_note-:9-9) Flavorings and colorings must be incorporated before setting, and the fluffy marshmallow mixture can be shaped by piping or transfer to a mold.[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) Finally, the marshmallow is allowed to cool and set into its final shape. Often starch") and/or powdered sugar are used to prevent sticking.[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8)[[12]](#cite_note-:4-12)

### Commercial

[edit | edit source]

Large-scale, commercial marshmallow manufacturing relies primarily on extrusion to aerate and shape the confection. The hot sugar mixture is forced through tubes under pressure, which incorporates air into the mixture and shapes it into ropes. These ropes are then typically cut into individual marshmallows.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8)[[10]](#cite_note-:10-10)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Marshmallows can have a long shelf life when properly stored. Exposure to the environment can cause marshmallows to dry out and become stale or melt together into a sticky clump. These marshmallows are still edible, but to preserve their original texture they should be kept in airtight packaging. If you find yourself with hardened stale marshmallows, try putting them in a ziplock bag with some slices of bread to gently soften.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Marshmallow is widely used across a range of different confections. For example, they may top cookies, be coated in chocolate or other toppings, filled with jam, incorporated into aice cream, and more.[[7]](#cite_note-:5-7) In the United States, marshmallows are toasted and combined with chocolate and graham crackers to make a dessert called s'mores.[[7]](#cite_note-:5-7)
